title: County-certified vs utility-approved testers
description: Why a county certification list, a city-approved list, and a non-endorsed handout are not the same thing even when they all help users find testers.
intro: Tester-list pages look deceptively similar. The problem is that the trust claim changes depending on whether the list comes from a county health agency, a utility approval program, or a non-endorsed public handout.
callout: This site has to keep those list types separate because they drive different wording, different route names, and different commercial constraints.
sourceExcerpt: A county-certified tester directory, a utility-approved roster, and a non-endorsed public handout may all be helpful, but they are not interchangeable trust contracts.

sections.heading: Utility-approved means the authority is stronger
sections.body: When a city or utility publishes its own approved or authorized tester list, the route can legitimately lead with the authority source and use approved-list language.
bullets: Santa Rosa and Scottsdale publish utility-controlled tester lists.
These pages can support approved-tester routes when the authority claim is actually justified.

sections.heading: County-certified is useful but not identical
sections.body: A county-certified tester directory can still be valuable, but it describes a different authority relationship. Pasadena is a strong example because the county list informs routing without automatically turning every local page into a utility-approved roster.
bullets: County-certified directories often deserve directory-only or carefully labeled routing.
The copy has to avoid implying city approval when the source is really county certification.

sections.heading: Public handouts can be usable without endorsement
sections.body: Some utilities publish a tester handout while explicitly saying they do not endorse the listed firms. That matters for route naming, label hierarchy, and directory separation.
bullets: Prescott Valley is a strong example of a list that helps users but does not justify an approved-list claim.
This is exactly why the site distinguishes approved-testers from find-a-tester.

sources.label: Los Angeles County certified tester directory
